danger_signals.visual_form: Colored blocks ascending toward top of playfield
danger_signals.meaning: Increasing pressure; blocks touching top = game over

danger_signals.visual_form: Faster upward movement of block stacks over time
danger_signals.meaning: Difficulty escalation; player losing control of pace

danger_signals.visual_form: Dense concentration of uncleared blocks occupying most screen space
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent loss state; player has limited moves remaining

safety_signals.visual_form: Blocks disappearing when three or more same-color blocks align
safety_signals.meaning: Successful action; player maintaining board control

safety_signals.visual_form: Cascading blocks falling and creating secondary matches
safety_signals.meaning: Strategic success; player leveraging game physics effectively

safety_signals.visual_form: Large clear areas with few blocks on screen
safety_signals.meaning: Player safety margin; breathing room to make next move

faction_indicators.color_identity: Varies by character/region (Fairies in JP Panel de Pon; Yoshi/Mario cast in NA Tetris Attack)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Character portrait/avatar displayed during Story Mode matchups
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Opponent character portraits shown as adversaries in Story Mode battles
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Character-specific background themes (Windy's background in JP version becomes Lakitu's background in NA version)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Fantasy fairy aesthetic (JP Panel de Pon) vs. Mario/Yoshi branding (NA Tetris Attack)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: No direct enemy faction; opponents are alternative characters from same cast
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Background art styles reflect either fantasy setting (JP) or Mushroom Kingdom locale (NA)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Slower block rise speed; blocks generated at lower frequency; simpler color palettes on screen
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Opponent AI makes slower/less effective moves; player has more time between block additions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Moderate block rise acceleration; more complex block patterns appearing; board fills progressively
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Opponent AI becomes more aggressive; block generation increases in pace

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rapid block rise; dense block concentration; visual feedback becomes more intense
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Opponent delivers large combos; player forced into reactive rather than proactive play; screen nearly full of blocks

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: All blocks cleared; opponent character defeated; transition animation to next stage background
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Progression to next opponent; environmental background changes signaling story advance

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Blocks reach top of playfield; game-over screen displays
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game halts; player cannot place further moves; match ends

environmental_storytelling.example: Each Story Mode opponent has unique background art that reflects their character identity (Windy fairy character has distinct background in JP version; replaced with Lakitu background in NA version)

environmental_storytelling.visual_form: Backgrounds change as player advances through Puzzle or Stage Clear modes, signaling progression through game world

environmental_storytelling.example: Fantasy fairy mythology setting (JP Panel de Pon) replaced with Mushroom Kingdom visual language (NA Tetris Attack) to communicate setting shift to international audiences
Different colored blocks serve as primary visual language; matching same colors creates narrative of order/harmony through alignment

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Continuous upward movement accelerates over time; indicates time pressure increasing on player
Selectable position moves across playfield; player can swap adjacent blocks to form matches
Disappear from screen when three or more align horizontally or vertically; blocks above fall downward
Executes block clearances on parallel playfield; displays portrait/character visual during battle sequences
Fall after matches clear; can trigger chain reactions if new matches form from falling blocks

notes: Panel de Pon communicates world-state and difficulty primarily through mechanical/temporal means (block rise speed, generation rate) rather than narrative systems. The game uses minimal faction complexity—characters function as cosmetic story mode opponents rather than representing opposing factions with distinct visual identities or political stakes. Environmental storytelling is limited to background art changes. Danger is communicated exclusively through board fill state and temporal pressure. No political or diplomatic communication systems exist; conflict is purely competitive (puzzle-solving race). Regional localization (JP fantasy vs. NA Mario universe) represents the only significant world-state communication through setting aesthetics.
